verdicts.question: What type of roof is present at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Material Storage photo (page 6) and Cable Routing / Proposed PV Area photos (pages 8-9) clearly show a flat, tiled concrete rooftop with no slope. Multiple rooftop photos confirm a level RCC slab surface with perimeter parapet walls.
verdicts.rationale: Multiple rooftop photos across pages 6, 8, and 9 consistently show a flat, horizontal RCC concrete roof surface, fully consistent with 'RCC Flat Roof'.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstructions on the roof that could affect the solar installation?
verdicts.designer_answer: details: Skylight on the roof that could affect the solar installation.
verdicts.photo_observation: SketchUp top-view layout on page 9 clearly shows a rectangular opening/skylight in the middle of the rooftop area, positioned between the two sections of the proposed panel array. The actual aerial rooftop photo on page 9 also shows a raised/framed feature consistent with a skylight in the proposed area.
verdicts.rationale: SketchUp top-view on page 9 clearly renders a skylight/opening in the roof plan positioned centrally within the panel zone; the aerial rooftop photo confirms an obstruction feature, consistent with the designer's stated skylight obstruction.

verdicts.question: What is the angle of the panels? (in degrees)
verdicts.photo_observation: SketchUp side-view models on pages 10-11 show an elevated structure with 2500mm front leg height. Structure detail on page 11 labels the front height as 2500mm with panel dimensions 2382mm (length) x 1133mm (width). For a 2382mm-long panel, a 10-degree tilt produces approximately 413mm height difference across the panel — consistent with the low-angle elevated structure visually depicted in the renders.
verdicts.rationale: SketchUp side-view and structure detail drawings on pages 10-11 show an elevated mounting structure with 2500mm front height that is visually consistent with approximately 10-degree panel tilt on a flat roof; no contradicting visual evidence.

verdicts.question: On how many roofs at the site are we installing solar?
verdicts.photo_observation: All rooftop photos (pages 6, 8, 9) and the SketchUp top-view model (page 9) show a single flat rooftop surface where all 10 panels are to be installed. No evidence of multiple separate roof levels or additional roof sections being used for the installation.
verdicts.rationale: Site photos and SketchUp layout consistently show all panels on a single roof section with no multiple installation areas visible.

verdicts.question: What type of mounting structure is planned?
verdicts.photo_observation: SketchUp side-view models on page 10 and structure detail drawings on page 11 clearly show an elevated mounting structure with tall vertical legs. The structure detail explicitly labels the front leg height as 2500mm (2.5m), directly confirming the HDGI elevated structure described by the designer.
verdicts.rationale: SketchUp structure detail on page 11 explicitly shows elevated mounting with 2500mm front height, directly matching the designer's answer of 'Elevated, HDGI with 2.5m front height'.

verdicts.question: What is the orientation of the solar panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: SketchUp top-view layout on page 9 includes a compass rose (N/S/E/W labeled) with all 10 panels positioned on the southern side of the rooftop, clearly facing south. The panel array direction in the top-view model is unambiguously aligned toward the South direction based on the compass indicator shown.
verdicts.rationale: SketchUp top-view on page 9 includes a compass and clearly shows panels oriented toward South, consistent with designer's answer.

verdicts.question: How can the roof be accessed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 contains a dedicated 'Staircase Details' section with a photo showing a covered indoor staircase with metal handrails leading upward; the step width is annotated as 0.5m. The Material Storage rooftop photo on page 6 also shows a staircase door/entrance at the rooftop level confirming this as the primary access route.
verdicts.rationale: Dedicated staircase photo on page 7 confirms indoor staircase access to the roof, consistent with designer's 'Staircase' answer.

verdicts.question: Is the building currently under construction?
verdicts.photo_observation: Building exterior photo on page 5 shows a completed, finished building with painted exterior walls, fitted windows, balconies, a metal gate, and parked vehicles. No scaffolding, exposed brick, bare concrete, or construction materials are visible.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior on page 5 shows a fully completed residential structure with no signs of ongoing construction, consistent with 'No'.

verdicts.question: What is the height of the building and how many floors does it have?
verdicts.designer_answer: Height of the building is 12m. it has G+3 floors.
verdicts.photo_observation: Building exterior photo on page 5 shows a multi-story residential building. Counting visible floors from street level: ground floor (gate/parking level), first floor (windows/balcony with railings), second floor (windows), and third floor (with wood-cladding facade element at top). This is consistent with G+3 (4 levels total). Side elevation photo on page 6 also shows multiple floor levels going upward.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior photos on pages 5-6 show approximately 4 visible floor levels (G+3) consistent with the designer's description; precise height of 12m cannot be confirmed visually but is plausible for G+3 at roughly 3m per floor.

verdicts.question: Is the site photo matching with the building description?
verdicts.photo_observation: Building exterior photo on page 5 shows a residential building at MEI Layout, Bengaluru. The building appears to match the project description: residential type, G+3 floors, complete construction. The flat RCC roof visible in other photos is consistent with the building type shown in the exterior photo.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior photo on page 5 shows a residential property consistent with the project description (residential, G+3, RCC flat roof); no contradictions between the photo and the building description.

verdicts.question: Is there an overhead HT line passing within a 10-meter radius of the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Building exterior photo (page 5) and material delivery route photos (pages 5-6) show the surrounding area. Only typical low-voltage distribution wires are visible in the street area; no large insulator-mounted high-tension conductors, HT pole hardware, or bundled HT conductors are visible near the building.
verdicts.rationale: Site photos show only standard low-voltage distribution wiring in the vicinity; no HT line infrastructure visible within the photographed area, consistent with 'No'.

verdicts.question: Can the delivery van/truck reach the material unloading point?
verdicts.photo_observation: Building exterior photo on page 5 shows a wide paved road in front of the property with an open metal gate and a vehicle (Bolero/Jeep type) clearly parked on the street, confirming vehicle access. The delivery route photos on pages 5-6 document the onsite material routing paths.
verdicts.rationale: Street-level photo on page 5 shows a paved road with a vehicle visible alongside the building, confirming delivery van/truck access to the site, consistent with 'Yes'.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstacles at the site that could hinder material lifting?
verdicts.photo_observation: Material Delivery Route photos on pages 5-6 show two separate routes: a narrow side passage (for small items) and an external route alongside the building (for large items like panels). Staircase photo on page 7 shows the indoor staircase with 0.5m step width annotation — narrow, but panels appear to use the external route rather than the staircase. No major fixed obstacles blocking lifting paths are visible.
verdicts.rationale: Two separate documented delivery routes accommodate both small and large items; no major fixed obstacles visible in the delivery route or staircase photos that would definitively hinder material lifting, consistent with 'No'.

verdicts.question: Did you discuss the location of the earth pits with the customer?
verdicts.designer_answer: Earth pits are located in the ground floor towards east direction.
verdicts.photo_observation: Earth Pit Location photo on page 13 shows a narrow ground-floor indoor/corridor area with 'LA Pit' and 'DC Earth Pit' labels marked on the floor. The area is at ground floor level. The east direction cannot be confirmed from the photo angle, but the ground floor placement is consistent.
verdicts.rationale: Earth pit location photo on page 13 confirms pit positions are marked at ground floor level with DC Earth Pit and LA Pit identified; ground floor location is consistent with the designer's description, though east-facing direction cannot be confirmed visually.

verdicts.question: Where is the Internet Router located?
verdicts.photo_observation: No specific photo of the internet router is present in the TSVR. The Observations section on page 13 states 'Wi-Fi upto solar meter is under customer scope,' consistent with the customer managing the router location.
verdicts.rationale: No site photo of the internet router location; stated as customer scope which is a logistical designation not visually verifiable from site photos.

verdicts.question: Did you pin the site location in your WhatsApp when you were at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: No photo evidence of a WhatsApp location pin is present in the document. This is a field action performed at the time of site visit and leaves no visual trace in the photo documentation.
verdicts.rationale: WhatsApp location pinning is a field action that leaves no visual trace in site photos; cannot be verified or contradicted from the TSVR photo documentation.

verdicts.question: What type of energy meter is currently installed?
verdicts.designer_answer: Phase: Three Phase, Type: Digital, If 3-phase: LT w/o CT (5 - 34.999)
verdicts.photo_observation: Meter Box photo on page 7 shows an open meter cubicle containing a meter unit with a flat rectangular digital display face, along with MCBs and wiring on the right side. The meter housing is consistent with a digital-type unit. Three-phase configuration and the LT w/o CT classification cannot be definitively read at photo resolution, but the meter form factor is consistent with a three-phase digital meter.
verdicts.rationale: Meter box photo on page 7 shows a digital-style meter (rectangular face with numeric display) consistent with a three-phase digital meter; photo resolution limits precise three-phase/CT verification but no visual contradictions to the stated meter type.

verdicts.question: Is the Meter cubicle photo matching with the previous answer?
verdicts.photo_observation: Meter Box photo on page 7 shows an open meter cubicle with a digital meter, MCBs, and associated wiring. The cubicle appearance is consistent with a three-phase digital meter installation as described; no obvious single-phase or analog meter characteristics are visible.
verdicts.rationale: Meter cubicle photo on page 7 shows components consistent with a three-phase digital meter panel, supporting the designer's 'Yes' for photo matching the stated meter type.

verdicts.question: Has the termination point in the Main meter panel been identified?
verdicts.photo_observation: Equipment Location photo on page 12 shows both 'Main meter' (red label) and 'Solar meter' (blue label) locations clearly marked with colored indicators on a wall panel. The main meter termination point and the proposed solar meter adjacency are explicitly identified in the photo.
verdicts.rationale: Equipment Location photo on page 12 clearly marks the main meter location and proposed solar meter position, confirming the termination point has been identified, consistent with 'Yes'.

verdicts.question: Is the Termination Point easily visible or sealed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Meter Box photo on page 7 shows the meter cubicle door open, with the meter and wiring clearly visible and accessible. The panel is an externally accessible open cubicle rather than a sealed or tamper-proof unit.
verdicts.rationale: Meter box photo on page 7 shows an open, accessible panel with visible meter and wiring, consistent with 'Visible' termination point.

verdicts.question: Is a Lightning Arrestor (LA) already available at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Rooftop photos (pages 6, 8, 9), meter box photo (page 7), and equipment location photo (page 12) show no existing lightning arrestor device (rod/spike) on the roof or connected to the electrical panel. Importantly, the Earth Pit Location photo on page 13 labels an 'LA Pit' as a planned future installation item, confirming the LA does not currently exist at the site.
verdicts.rationale: No existing lightning arrestor is visible in any site photo; the earth pit photo on page 13 labels 'LA Pit' as a new planned installation, confirming no existing LA at the site, consistent with 'No'.

verdicts.question: In case of an Enphase system, is the distance between the farthest micro-inverter and IQ Gateway > 50m?
verdicts.photo_observation: The building is G+3 with a stated height of 12m. The cable routing photo on page 8 shows the proposed cable path running vertically down the building exterior. For a compact residential G+3 building, the total cable run from the farthest rooftop microinverter down to the IQ Gateway location is plausibly well under 50m.
verdicts.rationale: G+3 building (12m height) with compact rooftop panel area; cable run from farthest rooftop microinverter to IQ Gateway is plausibly under 50m for this residential scale; no visual contradiction to stated 'Less than 50m'.

verdicts.question: In Main meter panel, is empty space available for solar meter?
verdicts.photo_observation: Meter box photo on page 7 shows the existing panel is fully occupied with the main meter, MCBs, and wiring with very limited free space. Equipment Location photo on page 12 confirms the solar meter is planned in a separate external location (blue 'Solar meter' label on a distinct wall area away from the main meter cubicle).
verdicts.rationale: Meter box photo (page 7) shows a congested panel with no obvious free space; Equipment Location photo (page 12) confirms solar meter is planned in a separate dedicated location, consistent with 'No' space available in the main panel.

verdicts.question: In Main meter panel, is empty space available to house the Enphase accessories or ACDB components?
verdicts.photo_observation: Meter box photo on page 7 shows the main panel already occupied with existing meter and MCBs. Equipment Location photo on page 12 confirms all new components (solar meter and associated accessories) are planned in separate dedicated spaces, not within the main panel.
verdicts.rationale: Main meter panel photo shows it is already full; Equipment Location photo confirms new equipment will be in separate locations; consistent with 'No' available space for Enphase accessories or ACDB.

verdicts.question: Is there space available to dig DC, AC and LA earth pits?
verdicts.photo_observation: Earth Pit Location photo on page 13 shows a highly constrained narrow indoor corridor at ground floor level with labeled 'LA Pit' and 'DC Earth Pit' positions. The space is a tiled indoor/entrance corridor with very limited available area — only designated pit spots are possible. No open soil or garden area for conventional pit digging is visible.
verdicts.rationale: Earth pit location photo shows a constrained narrow indoor corridor with only designated pit positions; consistent with 'No' freely available open ground for digging all three types of earth pits.

verdicts.question: In case of elevated structures, is an LA extension provided at the far end?
verdicts.photo_observation: SketchUp structure detail drawings on pages 10 and 11 show the elevated mounting structure with 2500mm front legs and the panel array layout. No LA extension rod or spike is depicted at the far end of the panel array in any of the 3D model drawings.
verdicts.rationale: SketchUp structure detail models on pages 10-11 show no LA extension element at the far end of the elevated panel array, consistent with designer's 'No' answer.

verdicts.question: In case of elevated structures, is it with or without grouting?
verdicts.photo_observation: SketchUp structure detail drawings on pages 10-11 show mounting leg bases with socket/anchor connection points at the roof slab level, which represent a grouted base design. The installation is pre-construction at the time of this TSVR, so no actual grouted footings are visible in site photos, but the design intent is clear from the 3D models.
verdicts.rationale: SketchUp design model on page 11 shows mounting legs with base anchor points at roof level consistent with a grouted installation design; 'With Grouting' reflects the planned construction method shown in the design drawings.

verdicts.question: Are there any challenges with respect to Installation and Maintenance activities?
verdicts.photo_observation: Site photos show standard rooftop access via a covered indoor staircase (page 7), adequate flat rooftop space with parapet walls (page 6), straightforward single-section panel layout (pages 8-9), and no unusual installation conditions (extreme heights without safety provisions, inaccessible areas, structural anomalies) are visible.
verdicts.rationale: Site photos show standard access and layout conditions with no visually obvious installation or maintenance challenges, consistent with 'No' challenges.

verdicts.question: Are there any safety risks associated with accessing the roof for installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Staircase photo on page 7 shows a covered indoor staircase with metal handrails providing safe vertical access. Rooftop photos on page 6 show perimeter parapet walls providing edge protection around the flat roof. No obvious safety hazards (exposed live conductors, unstable surfaces, missing barriers) are visible in any photo.
verdicts.rationale: Staircase photo shows covered, handrail-equipped indoor access; rooftop photos show perimeter parapet walls; no obvious safety risks visible in any photo, consistent with 'No' safety risks.

verdicts.question: Is space present in rooftop to store the panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Material Storage rooftop photo on page 6 shows a spacious flat rooftop with perimeter parapet walls of substantial height (approximately 1-1.2m visible). The walls provide surfaces against which panels could be leaned vertically for temporary storage during installation. Adequate wall length is visible around the rooftop perimeter.
verdicts.rationale: Rooftop photo on page 6 shows flat roof with substantial perimeter parapet walls suitable for vertically leaning panels; consistent with 'Vertically leaning against the wall' storage method.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimensions for solar meter cubicle placement (L x W of wall).
verdicts.photo_observation: Equipment Location photo on page 12 shows the solar meter location marked with a blue outline on an exterior wall. The wall area appears to have sufficient space for the solar meter cubicle, but no measurement scale or reference dimension is included in the photo to verify the stated 800mm x 499mm dimensions.
verdicts.rationale: Equipment location photo marks the solar meter wall area but includes no measurement references to verify the stated dimensions (L:800mm x W:499mm); requires physical site measurement confirmation.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimension for earth pits location (L x W of available space).
verdicts.photo_observation: Earth Pit Location photo on page 13 shows the pit locations marked in a narrow corridor, but no measurement scale or reference dimensions are included in the photo. The space appears constrained but the exact dimensions (450mm x 450mm) cannot be verified.
verdicts.rationale: Earth pit location photo shows the designated area but contains no measurement references to verify the stated 450mm x 450mm dimensions; physical measurement required for confirmation.

verdicts.question: Is LAN cable under customer scope?
verdicts.photo_observation: No specific LAN cable photo is present in the TSVR. The Observations section on page 13 states 'Wi-Fi upto solar meter is under customer scope,' which refers to Wi-Fi connectivity, not LAN cable specifically. The scope designation for LAN cable (EcoSoch vs customer) is a commercial/contractual item not verifiable from site photos.
verdicts.rationale: No site photo addresses LAN cable scope; observations mention only Wi-Fi scope; LAN cable responsibility is a contractual designation not visually verifiable from site photos.

verdicts.question: Is there any shadow that affects the solar installation?
verdicts.designer_notes: A small amount of shadow will fall on the panels.
verdicts.photo_observation: Shadow Analysis section on page 12 includes a SketchUp shadow simulation model with the explicit caption 'A small amount of Shadow will cast on the panels in the month of November and December.' The simulation (set to December 25, 14:37 UTC+05:30) clearly shows shadow falling on portions of the panel array from adjacent building parapet walls or water heater structures.
verdicts.rationale: SketchUp shadow simulation on page 12 explicitly demonstrates shadow on panels with a caption confirming 'A small amount of Shadow will cast on the panels in the month of November and December,' directly consistent with designer's 'Yes' and noted small shadow impact.

verdicts.question: Is there any trench work to be done to carry the AC cable to the meter location?
verdicts.photo_observation: Cable Routing photo on page 8 shows the proposed cable path using surface-mounted conduit routing along the building exterior wall (colored route lines indicating AC and DC cable paths running vertically down the wall face). Equipment location on page 12 shows the meter at a wall location reachable via surface routing. No ground-level trenching path is indicated in any photo or diagram.
verdicts.rationale: Cable routing diagrams on page 8 show surface-mounted conduit routing along building walls with no indication of ground trenching; consistent with 'No' trench work required.
